request.question: What artist created a traditional folk song among prisoners in the American South as well as Maybellene?

title: Sarie Marais
Here We à Go Go Again!
Contemporary folk music
On Top of Old Smoky
The House of the Rising Sun
Song So-hee
Folk Den
Billy Boy
Midnight Special (song)
Min'yō
sentences: "Sarie Marais" (also known as "My Sarie Marais" and pronounced "May SAH-ree mah-REH") is a traditional South African folk song, created during either the First Anglo-Boer War (c. 1880) (less likely) or the Second Anglo-Boer War (ca. 1900).

The tune was possibly taken from a song dating from the American Civil War called "Ellie Rhee" (itself perhaps a version of the traditional folk song "Foggy Dew"), with the words translated into Afrikaans.
Here We à Go Go Again!
is Johnny Rivers's second official album, and like his first album, "At the Whisky à Go Go", it was recorded live at the Whisky a Go Go in Los Angeles, California.
The album reached #38 on the Billboard Charts and spawned two hit singles: "Maybellene" and "Midnight Special".
Contemporary folk music refers to a wide variety of genres that emerged in the mid 20th century and afterwards which were associated with traditional folk music.
Starting in the mid-20th century a new form of popular folk music evolved from traditional folk music.
This process and period is called the (second) folk revival and reached a zenith in the 1960s.
The most common name for this new form of music is also "folk music", but is often called "contemporary folk music" or "folk revival music" to make the distinction.
The transition was somewhat centered in the US and is also called the American folk music revival.
Fusion genres such as folk rock, folktronica, and others also evolved within this phenomenon.

While contemporary folk music is a genre generally distinct from traditional folk music, it often shares the same English name, performers and venues as traditional folk music; even individual songs may be a blend of the two.
"On Top of Old Smokey" is a traditional folk song of the United States.
As recorded by The Weavers, the song reached the pop music charts in 1951.
It is catalogued as Roud Folk Song Index No. 414.
In one version the first verse is the following; for more on the words see below.
"The House of the Rising Sun" is a traditional folk song, sometimes called "Rising Sun Blues".
It tells of a life gone wrong in New Orleans; many versions also urge a sibling to avoid the same fate.
The most successful commercial version, recorded in 1964 by British rock group the Animals, was a number one hit on the UK Singles Chart and also in the United States and France.
As a traditional folk song recorded by an electric rock band, it has been described as the "first folk-rock hit".
Song So-hee (born October 20, 1997), is a South Korean singer who specializes in minyo, traditional Korean folk songs.
She has been active since childhood and has often been called a "young female master singer".
She became famous nationwide after she performed "Changbu Taryeong", a well-known Korean traditional folk song, at the KBS National Singing Contest.
She left a deep impression upon the viewers with her performance.
Since then she has been invited to perform at various events, both at home and abroad.
Currently she is a first-year college student at Dankook University (as a traditional music major) and successfully manages her life as a student and a performer.
Folk Den is a folk music website founded in 1995 by Roger McGuinn, former front man of The Byrds.
Hosted at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill's ibiblio, the site is intended to preserve and promote folk music and offers a new folk song on a monthly basis.
Each posting provides an MP3 of a traditional folk song along with a descriptive paragraph, lyrics, guitar chords and related images.
The site has received positive reviews from "The New York Times", the Discovery Channel, and CNET.
"Billy Boy" is a traditional folk song and nursery rhyme found in the United States.
It has a Roud Folk Song Index number of 326.
It is a variant of the traditional English folk song "My Boy Billy", collected by Ralph Vaughan Williams and published by him in 1912 as number 232 in "Novello's School Songs".
The song is very popular with the Orange Order.
"Midnight Special" is a traditional folk song thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South.
The title comes from the refrain, which refers to the passenger train "Midnight Special" and its "ever-loving light" (sometimes "ever-living light"):
Min'yō (民謡 ) is a genre of traditional Japanese music.
The term is a translation of the German word "Volkslied" (folk song) and has only been in use since the twentieth century.

Japanese traditional designations referring to more or less the same genre include "inaka bushi" ("country song") "inaka buri" ("country tune"), "hina uta" ("rural song") and the like, but for most of the people who sang such songs they were simply "uta" (song).

The term min'yō is now sometimes also used to refer to traditional songs of other countries, though a preceding adjective is needed: Furansu min'yō = French folk song; for this reason, many sources in Japanese also feel the need to preface the term with "Nihon": Nihon min'yō = Japanese [traditional] folk song.

title: Here We à Go Go Again!
Here We à Go Go Again!

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

gold_support_titles: Here We à Go Go Again!
response.hop1_query: What artist created a traditional folk song among prisoners in the American South as well as Maybellene?
hop1_docs: Midnight Special (song) | "Midnight Special" is a traditional folk song thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South.  The title comes from the refrain, which refers to the passenger train "Midnight Special" and its "ever-loving light" (sometimes "ever-living light"):

Song Chang-sik | Song Chang-Sik (born 2 February, 1947) is a South Korean folk singer-songwriter.  He is well known for denying the traditional Solfège (both Korean and Western), and re-interpreting the melody on his own.  These melodies can be hardly written with traditional note system, as he "creates" notes that never existed.b

Sarie Marais | "Sarie Marais" (also known as "My Sarie Marais" and pronounced "May SAH-ree mah-REH") is a traditional South African folk song, created during either the First Anglo-Boer War (c. 1880) (less likely) or the Second Anglo-Boer War (ca. 1900).  The tune was possibly taken from a song dating from the American Civil War called "Ellie Rhee" (itself perhaps a version of the traditional folk song "Foggy Dew"), with the words translated into Afrikaans.

The Hollow Trees | The Hollow Trees play what they call “folk music for Families.”  It’s high energy acoustic Americana music played with passion, skill, and humor.  They combine the musical styles of bluegrass music, country music, folk music, blues and jazz and put their stamp on many traditional folk songs as well as original compositions.

Folsom Prison Blues | "Folsom Prison Blues" is a song written in 1953 and first recorded in 1955 by American singer-songwriter Johnny Cash.  The song combines elements from two popular folk styles, the train song and the prison song, both of which Cash continued to use for the rest of his career.  It was one of Cash's signature songs.  It was the eleventh track on his debut album "With His Hot and Blue Guitar" and it was also included (same version) on "All Aboard the Blue Train".  A live version, recorded among inmates at Folsom State Prison itself, became a #1 hit on the country music charts in 1968.

Down in the Valley (folk song) | "Down in the Valley", also known as "Birmingham Jail", is a traditional country-blues American folk song.  It has been recorded by many artists, and is included in the "Songs of Expanding America" recordings in the Burl Ives six-album set "Historical America in Song".

You Are My Little Bird | You Are My Little Bird is an album by Elizabeth Mitchell, released in 2006 by Smithsonian Folkways Recordings.  The album is a collection of children's music played in a folk music style.  In addition to versions of folk tunes, it features covers of a variety of songs by other artists, among them "What Goes On" by The Velvet Underground, Neil Young's "Little Wing", and "Three Little Birds" by Bob Marley.  On most of the songs, Mitchell is accompanied by family members.  The liner notes include personal stories from the artist, but no lyrics.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Midnight Special — passage [1] "Midnight Special" : "a traditional folk song thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South."
  - Maybellene — not mentioned in the supplied passages.
  - No artist credited in the provided passages for either song.

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - "Created" is ambiguous: does it mean "wrote/composed" (author) or "recorded/performed" (artist)? 
  - "Midnight Special" is traditional (no single credited creator), so a single creator may not exist.
  - The passage set lacks the "Maybellene" page and any artist information (e.g., Chuck Berry).

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Author/composer and original artist/recording credits for "Maybellene".
  - Any artist who is credited with creating or notably recording both "Midnight Special" and "Maybellene" (or confirmation that no single artist created both).

- retrieval_direction (priority):
  1. "Maybellene (song)" page — to get writer/artist and release info.
  2. "Chuck Berry" page — (Chuck Berry is widely credited for "Maybellene") to confirm authorship/recording.
  3. "Midnight Special (song)" page (full article) — to check earliest known recordings and any credited arrangers/artists.
  4. Search: "artist who recorded both 'Midnight Special' and 'Maybellene'" or "covers 'Midnight Special' list" to see if any single performer recorded both.
response.hop2_query: Midnight Special Maybellene artist recorded both
hop2_docs: Here We à Go Go Again! | Here We à Go Go Again!  is Johnny Rivers's second official album, and like his first album, "At the Whisky à Go Go", it was recorded live at the Whisky a Go Go in Los Angeles, California.  The album reached #38 on the Billboard Charts and spawned two hit singles: "Maybellene" and "Midnight Special".

Midnight Shift Records | Midnight Shift Records is a record label and events organisation established in 2009 and based out of Singapore and Berlin.  The label specializes in Techno and House music from both emerging and established producers and has been recognized as a label to note out of Asia and one of Southeast Asia's top labels.

Maybellene | "Maybellene" is one of the first rock and roll songs.  It was written and recorded in 1955 by Chuck Berry, and inspired/adapted from the Western Swing fiddle tune "Ida Red," which was recorded in 1938 by Bob Wills and his Texas Playboys.  Berry's song tells the story of a hot rod race and a broken romance.  It was released in July 1955 as a single by Chess Records, of Chicago, Illinois.  It was Berry's first single and his first hit.  "Maybellene" is considered one of the pioneering rock songs: "Rolling Stone" magazine wrote, "Rock & roll guitar starts here."  The record is an early instance of the complete rock-and-roll package: youthful subject matter; a small, guitar-driven combo; clear diction; and an atmosphere of unrelenting excitement.  The lyrics describe a man driving a V8 Ford chasing his unfaithful girlfriend in her Cadillac Coupe DeVille.

Midnight Special (Jimmy Smith album) | Midnight Special is an album by American jazz organist Jimmy Smith featuring performances recorded in 1960 and released on the Blue Note label.  The album was recorded at the same session which produced "Back at the Chicken Shack" (1960).

Midnight Special (Uncle Kracker album) | Midnight Special is the fifth studio album by Uncle Kracker and his first full-length country album.  It was released November 20, 2012 under Sugar Hill Records.

Midnight Special (Harry Belafonte album) | Midnight Special is a 1962 album by Jamaican-American singer, Harry Belafonte.  The album notably contains the very first official recording of Bob Dylan, who plays harmonica on the title track.

Christmas After Midnight | Christmas After Midnight is the first Christmas album, and the sixth studio album, by American recording artist Fantasia.  It will be released by Concord Records on October 6, 2017, in the United States.
